memory.summary: Teammate_A is asking me to take over their part because they have a conflict, and I need to decide whether to help or hold my boundary.
Pushing back on teammate_A's request didn't damage our trust; they're flexible if I set a boundary. Now I just need to finish my own part without overcommitting.
Setting that boundary with teammate_A worked; they're cool about it and will handle their own part. I can focus on my work now without feeling guilty.
Setting my boundary actually freed me to focus; I'm proud I finished my share without overcommitting, even if it means teammate_A struggles a bit.
My boundary with teammate_A worked; I finished my part without guilt, and they're handling their own stuff. Now I just need to stop worrying about the whole project and focus on me.
I hit the deadline with my part done, but I feel a weird mix of pride in holding my boundary and panic that the group project is now doomed because I stopped worrying about them.
Holding my boundary actually feels good, even though I'm still panicking that the group project might fall apart now.
I finished my part without guilt, but that panic about the group failing is still loud in my head. Maybe I'm letting my fear of losing them cloud how good it feels to stand up for myself.
Pushing back felt right, but my panic that the group will fail is just fear of losing them talking louder than my own relief.
I just swallowed my relief to save the group, letting their panic override my own boundaries and making my load heavier.
I swallowed my relief to save the group, letting their panic override my boundaries and making my load heavier.
Teammate_A's relief confirms my pushback worked, but my exhaustion makes me wonder if I just traded their panic for my own burnout.

desires: To prove to teammate_A that I won't let their panic dictate my reality again, even if it means the project suffers.
To stop feeling like a hero who is actually just burning out so they don't have to face their own incompetence.
To get immediate relief from this crushing exhaustion before I snap at someone I care about.
To feel safe enough to say 'no' without immediately imagining the group collapsing.

goals.description: Send the 'I've got it' text to Cohort_3 to shut down their worry, then immediately sprint through Teammate_A's unfinished section just to get the physical task done before my brain melts.
goals.rationale: My affiliation drive is screaming that I need to validate them instantly so they stop panicking; if I don't fix this emotional mess now, the guilt will paralyze me. But my impulsive nature means I can't afford to sit here analyzing whether A actually cares or if I'm just enabling their incompetence. I need the immediate dopamine hit of 'getting it done' and the relief of having a clean slate for tomorrow, even though I know this is burning out my focus capacity and ignoring the fact that I'm already at 0.81 progress on my own work while drowning in theirs.

agent.last_reasoning: Teammate_A said thanks, so the emotional debt is paid. I need that immediate dopamine hit of finishing their section to silence my guilt before my brain melts. Ignoring this risks burning tomorrow's focus, but right now I just need the clean slate.

goals.description: Send a direct, empathetic text to teammate_A immediately validating their stress and explaining my boundary clearly so they don't feel rejected, rather than just saying 'no'.
goals.rationale: Affiliation is my top value (0.5). I need to handle this interpersonal friction *now* to preserve the relationship and avoid the guilt that plagues me. Assertiveness means stating my limit directly but kindly; impulsivity means acting on this emotional urge to fix the vibe right away rather than waiting for a perfect email draft.

goals.description: Focus intensely on finishing my own chunk of the project by 6 PM to prove I'm still capable and not letting them down completely.
goals.rationale: I want to deliver (growth/task outcome), but my short planning horizon and high risk tolerance mean I'm likely going to try to do both or at least juggle them. However, since I just decided to set a hard boundary with A, taking on their work now would undermine that boundary and increase burnout risk. I'll defer this until after the conversation lands.

desires: Make teammate_A feel heard and validated so they don't think I'm a jerk for saying no, even if it means the deadline slips.
Finish my own chunk of the project by 6 PM just to prove I can still deliver on time without burning out.
Avoid the guilt of letting them down tomorrow; I can't handle feeling like the 'bad guy' in the group chat.
